text: A Thai female, fifty-three years old, presented with right kidney mass for medical check-up. CT scan showed ill-defined and undetermined right kidney mass, size four centimeters.

During the laparoscopic partial nephrectomy, the liver is immobilized, and traction is applied to the diaphragm using an instrument from the fourth port. The right colon is mobilized and dissected to access the retroperitoneal space. The right ureter and lower pole of the kidney are dissected, with the ureter held by a bipolar instrument in the surgeon's left hand. The kidney hilum is dissected, separating the renal artery from the hilum. The renal vein is also dissected, and a vascular loop is inserted around it.

The surgeon dissects the tumor from the lower pole of the kidney, marking the line of dissection with electrocautery. The lower pole is mobilized free from the gelatofascia, and the renal artery is clamped after the tumor is excised from the kidney.

The surgeon completes the first layer of suturing using a V-Lock suture, ensuring there is no active bleeding. Hem-o-lok clips are applied to maintain tension without tying knots, aiming to reduce warm ischemic time.

The surgeon unclamps the artery after ensuring no active bleeding. A second layer of suture is applied using Vicryl-IIO, with hemlock clips used for stitch tension instead of tying knots. Surgical cell material is placed over the sutures to further control bleeding.
The surgeon removes the vascular loop and retrieves the excised kidney specimen into a specimen bag.

Results There was no perioperative complication Operative time was two hours The warm ischemic time was twenty-two minutes Estimate blood loss was forty-five sixty Hospital length of stay was three days The pathology was angiomyolipoma size four centimeters There was no malignancy
